method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
Howland Island's flora is highly sparse due to its arid, equatorial climate, consisting primarily of grasses, prostrate vines, and low-growing shrubs.[1] The island lacks natural freshwater resources, which has prevented the establishment of larger trees, despite historical attempts by human settlers to plant them.[1] As a designated National Wildlife Refuge, the island provides critical nesting, roosting, and foraging habitat for numerous species of seabirds and shorebirds, including boobies, terns, and frigatebirds.[1] The surrounding marine environment encompasses extensive coral reefs that support a diverse array of fish and marine invertebrates.[1] Historically, invasive species such as feral cats and rats severely impacted the native bird populations, but successful eradication programs in the late 20th century have allowed the island's avian ecosystem to recover.[1]

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, meaning it does not possess a traditional local economy.[1] Historically, the United States claimed the atoll in 1857 under the Guano Act of 1856, but the island's guano deposits are now completely exhausted.[1] In the 1930s, the island provided some utility as a stopover for airplanes flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1] 

Today, economic activity is heavily restricted because the island is a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and was designated as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009.[1] Howland Island has an Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) that is dominated by a sediment-covered abyssal plain and includes several Cretaceous seamounts.[2][5] While commercial activity is minimal, the U.S. EEZ surrounding the Pacific Remote Island Areas, which includes Howland Island, has accounted for approximately 2 percent of pelagic species deployments in the region.[3] Overall, there is a notable lack of comprehensive socioeconomic data available for the island and its surrounding territories.[5]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is located in the UTC-12:00 time zone, which is also designated by the nautical letter Y (Yankee).[1][2] It shares this time zone with neighboring Baker Island as part of the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[1][2] Because this time zone is on the Western Hemisphere side of the International Date Line, also known as the IDLW time zone, Howland Island is one of the last places on Earth to experience the end of a day.[3][5] The UTC-12:00 time zone is entirely uninhabited.[3][5] Due to its status as the latest place where any given date remains valid, the island is used to establish the "Anywhere on Earth" (AoE) calendar designation.[3] A deadline expires Anywhere on Earth when the day officially ends on Howland Island.[3] Furthermore, there is a 26-hour time difference between Howland Island and the territories of Kiribati.[1]

==History==
There is evidence of an early Polynesian presence on Howland Island.[4] The island's entrance into recorded history occurred in 1791 when it was discovered by American sailor Edmund Fanning.[1] The first recorded sighting by a whaling ship was in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket captain who initially named it Worth Island.[2] Several American whaling vessels sighted and charted the area during this period.[4] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the whaleship *Isabella* visited the atoll and renamed it Howland Island in honor of the ship's lookout.[2][4]

The United States formally claimed the island in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[1][2][4] Captain Netcher formed the United States Guano Company and sent a vessel to the island in 1859, but the American Guano Company had already occupied the territory.[4] A legal dispute between the two enterprises was settled in court in 1865, granting both companies permission to operate.[4] British and American interests extracted organic nitrate until the operations ceased in 1878.[3][4]

To establish a permanent United States presence in the Central Pacific, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panala‘au Project, arrived from Hawaii in 1935.[2][4] The initial group consisted of a rotating team of four male students and alumni from Honolulu's Kamehameha School.[4] They built a settlement named Itascatown, honoring the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter *Itasca*.[4] The island was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936.[2] On July 2, 1937, the American aviator Amelia Earhart and her navigator, Frederick J. Noonan, disappeared near Howland Island.[2] They had departed from Lae, Papua New Guinea, and were attempting to reach the island during Earhart's circumnavigational flight around the world.[1][2]

During World War II, the island was utilized as a lookout point by the U.S. military.[1] In later decades, ecological management became a priority; feral cats that were introduced to the island in 1982 were eradicated by humans in 1986 to prevent destructive predation on local seabird and shorebird species.[4] The site's conservation history culminated in its 2009 designation as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, originally encompassing the island's landmass and surrounding waters out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge is managed as a strict nature reserve where public access, fishing, mining, and other potentially harmful activities are prohibited.[2][5] Following the creation of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument by President George W. Bush in 2009, the refuge's boundaries were expanded to 12 nautical miles.[4] The broader monument waters, which extend out to 50 nautical miles, are cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).[4] 

The protected area supports a high biomass of planktivores and piscivores, alongside significant populations of species protected under the Endangered Species Act.[4] Notably, the refuge contains the highest densities of green sea turtles recorded anywhere in the U.S. Pacific.[4] To maintain and enhance the ecosystem, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service conducts biological surveys and monitors the island's wildlife.[2][5] Management plans for the refuge include initiatives to attract two rare seabird species, increase scientific research opportunities, and potentially establish a rotating year-round field camp to serve as a baseline site for global climate change research.[5]

==Earhart Light==
The Earhart Light is a small lighthouse that was constructed on the island in 1937 in anticipation of Amelia Earhart's scheduled refueling stop.[3] Shortly after the attack on Pearl Harbor in December 1941, Japanese aircraft bombed the island and strafed the lighthouse.[4][5] Additionally, a Japanese submarine fired shells into the adjacent settlement, rendering the light beacon useless.[2] During these attacks, a plaque on the lighthouse that honored Earhart was shattered by machine-gun bullets.[5]

The station has remained inactive since 1942, and the lighthouse was ultimately abandoned.[3][4] Although it was partially destroyed during World War II, the structure was rebuilt in the early 1960s.[4] During brief subsequent visits to the island, the beacon was restored and updated to include the names of the two colonists who died during the Japanese bombings.[2] Today, the unlit structure serves as a navigational day beacon.[3][5]

==Image gallery==
Astronauts aboard the International Space Station photograph Howland Island and numerous other reefs worldwide as part of a global monitoring and mapping program.[2][4] High-resolution images from this program are used to assess reef ecosystem health, update geographic maps, and calculate the bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.[2][4] A notable photograph of the island was acquired on December 3, 2004, by an Expedition 10 crewmember from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km).[2][3] Captured with a Kodak 760C digital camera and an 800 mm lens, the image shows the island's coral fragments and the white breakers that indicate its active fringing reef.[2][4]

<gallery>
File:Howland island nasa.jpg|NASA astronaut image of Howland Island captured on December 3, 2004, showing the encircling white breakers of its fringing reef.[2][3]
</gallery>
